speaker: CAESAR
text: The ides of March are come.

speaker: SOOTHSAYER
text: Ay, Caesar, but not gone.

speaker: ARTEMIDORUS
text: Hail, Caesar. Read this schedule.

speaker: DECIUS
text: Trebonius doth desire you to o’erread,

speaker: DECIUS
text: At your best leisure, this his humble suit.

speaker: ARTEMIDORUS
text: O Caesar, read mine first, for mine’s a suit

speaker: ARTEMIDORUS
text: That touches Caesar nearer. Read it, great Caesar.

speaker: CAESAR
text: What touches us ourself shall be last served.

speaker: ARTEMIDORUS
text: Delay not, Caesar; read it instantly.

speaker: CAESAR
text: What, is the fellow mad?

speaker: PUBLIUS
text: Sirrah, give place.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: What, urge you your petitions in the street?

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Come to the Capitol.

speaker: POPILIUS
text: I wish your enterprise today may thrive.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: What enterprise, Popilius?

speaker: POPILIUS
text: Fare you well.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: What said Popilius Lena?

speaker: CASSIUS
text: He wished today our enterprise might thrive.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: I fear our purpose is discoverèd.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Look how he makes to Caesar. Mark him.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Casca, be sudden, for we fear prevention.—

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Brutus, what shall be done? If this be known,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Cassius or Caesar never shall turn back,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: For I will slay myself.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Cassius, be constant.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Popilius Lena speaks not of our purposes,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: For look, he smiles, and Caesar doth not change.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Trebonius knows his time, for look you, Brutus,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: He draws Mark Antony out of the way.

speaker: DECIUS
text: Where is Metellus Cimber? Let him go

speaker: DECIUS
text: And presently prefer his suit to Caesar.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: He is addressed. Press near and second him.

speaker: CINNA
text: Casca, you are the first that rears your hand.

speaker: CAESAR
text: Are we all ready? What is now amiss

speaker: CAESAR
text: That Caesar and his Senate must redress?

speaker: METELLUS
text: Most high, most mighty, and most puissant Caesar,

speaker: METELLUS
text: Metellus Cimber throws before thy seat

speaker: METELLUS
text: An humble heart.

speaker: CAESAR
text: I must prevent thee, Cimber.

speaker: CAESAR
text: These couchings and these lowly courtesies

speaker: CAESAR
text: Might fire the blood of ordinary men

speaker: CAESAR
text: And turn preordinance and first decree

speaker: CAESAR
text: Into the law of children. Be not fond

speaker: CAESAR
text: To think that Caesar bears such rebel blood

speaker: CAESAR
text: That will be thawed from the true quality

speaker: CAESAR
text: With that which melteth fools—I mean sweet words,

speaker: CAESAR
text: Low-crookèd curtsies, and base spaniel fawning.

speaker: CAESAR
text: Thy brother by decree is banishèd.

speaker: CAESAR
text: If thou dost bend and pray and fawn for him,

speaker: CAESAR
text: I spurn thee like a cur out of my way.

speaker: CAESAR
text: Know: Caesar doth not wrong, nor without cause

speaker: CAESAR
text: Will he be satisfied.

speaker: METELLUS
text: Is there no voice more worthy than my own

speaker: METELLUS
text: To sound more sweetly in great Caesar’s ear

speaker: METELLUS
text: For the repealing of my banished brother?

speaker: BRUTUS
text: I kiss thy hand, but not in flattery, Caesar,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Desiring thee that Publius Cimber may

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Have an immediate freedom of repeal.

speaker: CAESAR
text: What, Brutus?

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Pardon, Caesar; Caesar, pardon!

speaker: CASSIUS
text: As low as to thy foot doth Cassius fall

speaker: CASSIUS
text: To beg enfranchisement for Publius Cimber.

speaker: CAESAR
text: I could be well moved, if I were as you.

speaker: CAESAR
text: If I could pray to move, prayers would move me.

speaker: CAESAR
text: But I am constant as the Northern Star,

speaker: CAESAR
text: Of whose true fixed and resting quality

speaker: CAESAR
text: There is no fellow in the firmament.

speaker: CAESAR
text: The skies are painted with unnumbered sparks;

speaker: CAESAR
text: They are all fire, and every one doth shine.

speaker: CAESAR
text: But there’s but one in all doth hold his place.

speaker: CAESAR
text: So in the world: ’tis furnished well with men,

speaker: CAESAR
text: And men are flesh and blood, and apprehensive.

speaker: CAESAR
text: Yet in the number I do know but one

speaker: CAESAR
text: That unassailable holds on his rank,

speaker: CAESAR
text: Unshaked of motion; and that I am he

speaker: CAESAR
text: Let me a little show it, even in this:

speaker: CAESAR
text: That I was constant Cimber should be banished

speaker: CAESAR
text: And constant do remain to keep him so.

speaker: CINNA
text: O Caesar—

speaker: CAESAR
text: Hence. Wilt thou lift up Olympus?

speaker: DECIUS
text: Great Caesar—

speaker: CAESAR
text: Doth not Brutus bootless kneel?

speaker: CASCA
text: Speak, hands, for me!

speaker: CAESAR
text: Et tu, Brutè?—Then fall, Caesar.

speaker: CINNA
text: Liberty! Freedom! Tyranny is dead!

speaker: CINNA
text: Run hence, proclaim, cry it about the streets.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Some to the common pulpits and cry out

speaker: CASSIUS
text: “Liberty, freedom, and enfranchisement.”

speaker: BRUTUS
text: People and Senators, be not affrighted.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Fly not; stand still. Ambition’s debt is paid.

speaker: CASCA
text: Go to the pulpit, Brutus.

speaker: DECIUS
text: And Cassius too.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Where’s Publius?

speaker: CINNA
text: Here, quite confounded with this mutiny.

speaker: METELLUS
text: Stand fast together, lest some friend of Caesar’s

speaker: METELLUS
text: Should chance—

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Talk not of standing.—Publius, good cheer.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: There is no harm intended to your person,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Nor to no Roman else. So tell them, Publius.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And leave us, Publius, lest that the people,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Rushing on us, should do your age some mischief.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Do so, and let no man abide this deed

speaker: BRUTUS
text: But we the doers.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Where is Antony?

speaker: TREBONIUS
text: Fled to his house amazed.

speaker: TREBONIUS
text: Men, wives, and children stare, cry out, and run

speaker: TREBONIUS
text: As it were doomsday.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Fates, we will know your pleasures.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: That we shall die we know; ’tis but the time,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: And drawing days out, that men stand upon.

speaker: CASCA
text: Why, he that cuts off twenty years of life

speaker: CASCA
text: Cuts off so many years of fearing death.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Grant that, and then is death a benefit.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: So are we Caesar’s friends, that have abridged

speaker: BRUTUS
text: His time of fearing death. Stoop, Romans, stoop,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: And let us bathe our hands in Caesar’s blood

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Up to the elbows and besmear our swords.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Then walk we forth, even to the marketplace,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: And, waving our red weapons o’er our heads,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Let’s all cry “Peace, freedom, and liberty!”

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Stoop then, and wash.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: How many ages hence

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Shall this our lofty scene be acted over

speaker: CASSIUS
text: In states unborn and accents yet unknown!

speaker: BRUTUS
text: How many times shall Caesar bleed in sport,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: That now on Pompey’s basis lies along

speaker: BRUTUS
text: No worthier than the dust!

speaker: CASSIUS
text: So oft as that shall be,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: So often shall the knot of us be called

speaker: CASSIUS
text: The men that gave their country liberty.

speaker: DECIUS
text: What, shall we forth?

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Ay, every man away.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Brutus shall lead, and we will grace his heels

speaker: CASSIUS
text: With the most boldest and best hearts of Rome.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Soft, who comes here? A friend of Antony’s.

speaker: SERVANT
text: Thus, Brutus, did my master bid me kneel.

speaker: SERVANT
text: Thus did Mark Antony bid me fall down,

speaker: SERVANT
text: And, being prostrate, thus he bade me say:

speaker: SERVANT
text: Brutus is noble, wise, valiant, and honest;

speaker: SERVANT
text: Caesar was mighty, bold, royal, and loving.

speaker: SERVANT
text: Say, I love Brutus, and I honor him;

speaker: SERVANT
text: Say, I feared Caesar, honored him, and loved him.

speaker: SERVANT
text: If Brutus will vouchsafe that Antony

speaker: SERVANT
text: May safely come to him and be resolved

speaker: SERVANT
text: How Caesar hath deserved to lie in death,

speaker: SERVANT
text: Mark Antony shall not love Caesar dead

speaker: SERVANT
text: So well as Brutus living, but will follow

speaker: SERVANT
text: The fortunes and affairs of noble Brutus

speaker: SERVANT
text: Thorough the hazards of this untrod state

speaker: SERVANT
text: With all true faith. So says my master Antony.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Thy master is a wise and valiant Roman.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: I never thought him worse.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Tell him, so please him come unto this place,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: He shall be satisfied and, by my honor,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Depart untouched.

speaker: SERVANT
text: I’ll fetch him presently.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: I know that we shall have him well to friend.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: I wish we may; but yet have I a mind

speaker: CASSIUS
text: That fears him much, and my misgiving still

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Falls shrewdly to the purpose.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: But here comes Antony.—Welcome, Mark Antony!

speaker: ANTONY
text: O mighty Caesar, dost thou lie so low?

speaker: ANTONY
text: Are all thy conquests, glories, triumphs, spoils

speaker: ANTONY
text: Shrunk to this little measure? Fare thee well.—

speaker: ANTONY
text: I know not, gentlemen, what you intend,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Who else must be let blood, who else is rank.

speaker: ANTONY
text: If I myself, there is no hour so fit

speaker: ANTONY
text: As Caesar’s death’s hour, nor no instrument

speaker: ANTONY
text: Of half that worth as those your swords made rich

speaker: ANTONY
text: With the most noble blood of all this world.

speaker: ANTONY
text: I do beseech you, if you bear me hard,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Now, whilst your purpled hands do reek and smoke,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Fulfill your pleasure. Live a thousand years,

speaker: ANTONY
text: I shall not find myself so apt to die;

speaker: ANTONY
text: No place will please me so, no mean of death,

speaker: ANTONY
text: As here by Caesar, and by you cut off,

speaker: ANTONY
text: The choice and master spirits of this age.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: O Antony, beg not your death of us!

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Though now we must appear bloody and cruel,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: As by our hands and this our present act

speaker: BRUTUS
text: You see we do, yet see you but our hands

speaker: BRUTUS
text: And this the bleeding business they have done.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Our hearts you see not; they are pitiful;

speaker: BRUTUS
text: And pity to the general wrong of Rome

speaker: BRUTUS
text: (As fire drives out fire, so pity pity)

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Hath done this deed on Caesar. For your part,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: To you our swords have leaden points, Mark Antony.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Our arms in strength of malice, and our hearts

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Of brothers’ temper, do receive you in

speaker: BRUTUS
text: With all kind love, good thoughts, and reverence.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Your voice shall be as strong as any man’s

speaker: CASSIUS
text: In the disposing of new dignities.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Only be patient till we have appeased

speaker: BRUTUS
text: The multitude, beside themselves with fear;

speaker: BRUTUS
text: And then we will deliver you the cause

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Why I, that did love Caesar when I struck him,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Have thus proceeded.

speaker: ANTONY
text: I doubt not of your wisdom.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Let each man render me his bloody hand.

speaker: ANTONY
text: First, Marcus Brutus, will I shake with you.—

speaker: ANTONY
text: Next, Caius Cassius, do I take your hand.—

speaker: ANTONY
text: Now, Decius Brutus, yours;—now yours, Metellus;—

speaker: ANTONY
text: Yours, Cinna;—and, my valiant Casca, yours;—

speaker: ANTONY
text: Though last, not least in love, yours, good Trebonius.—

speaker: ANTONY
text: Gentlemen all—alas, what shall I say?

speaker: ANTONY
text: My credit now stands on such slippery ground

speaker: ANTONY
text: That one of two bad ways you must conceit me,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Either a coward or a flatterer.—

speaker: ANTONY
text: That I did love thee, Caesar, O, ’tis true!

speaker: ANTONY
text: If then thy spirit look upon us now,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Shall it not grieve thee dearer than thy death

speaker: ANTONY
text: To see thy Antony making his peace,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Shaking the bloody fingers of thy foes—

speaker: ANTONY
text: Most noble!—in the presence of thy corpse?

speaker: ANTONY
text: Had I as many eyes as thou hast wounds,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Weeping as fast as they stream forth thy blood,

speaker: ANTONY
text: It would become me better than to close

speaker: ANTONY
text: In terms of friendship with thine enemies.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Pardon me, Julius! Here wast thou bayed, brave hart,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Here didst thou fall, and here thy hunters stand

speaker: ANTONY
text: Signed in thy spoil and crimsoned in thy Lethe.

speaker: ANTONY
text: O world, thou wast the forest to this hart,

speaker: ANTONY
text: And this indeed, O world, the heart of thee.

speaker: ANTONY
text: How like a deer strucken by many princes

speaker: ANTONY
text: Dost thou here lie!

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Mark Antony—

speaker: ANTONY
text: Pardon me, Caius Cassius.

speaker: ANTONY
text: The enemies of Caesar shall say this;

speaker: ANTONY
text: Then, in a friend, it is cold modesty.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: I blame you not for praising Caesar so.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: But what compact mean you to have with us?

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Will you be pricked in number of our friends,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Or shall we on and not depend on you?

speaker: ANTONY
text: Therefore I took your hands, but was indeed

speaker: ANTONY
text: Swayed from the point by looking down on Caesar.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Friends am I with you all and love you all,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Upon this hope, that you shall give me reasons

speaker: ANTONY
text: Why and wherein Caesar was dangerous.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Or else were this a savage spectacle.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Our reasons are so full of good regard

speaker: BRUTUS
text: That were you, Antony, the son of Caesar,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: You should be satisfied.

speaker: ANTONY
text: That’s all I seek;

speaker: ANTONY
text: And am, moreover, suitor that I may

speaker: ANTONY
text: Produce his body to the marketplace,

speaker: ANTONY
text: And in the pulpit, as becomes a friend,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Speak in the order of his funeral.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: You shall, Mark Antony.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Brutus, a word with you.

speaker: CASSIUS
text:
Aside
                 
                to
                 
                Brutus
                .
                 
              You know not what you do. Do not consent

speaker: CASSIUS
text: That Antony speak in his funeral.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Know you how much the people may be moved

speaker: CASSIUS
text: By that which he will utter?

speaker: BRUTUS
text: By your pardon,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: I will myself into the pulpit first

speaker: BRUTUS
text: And show the reason of our Caesar’s death.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: What Antony shall speak I will protest

speaker: BRUTUS
text: He speaks by leave and by permission,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: And that we are contented Caesar shall

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Have all true rites and lawful ceremonies.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: It shall advantage more than do us wrong.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: I know not what may fall. I like it not.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Mark Antony, here, take you Caesar’s body.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: You shall not in your funeral speech blame us

speaker: BRUTUS
text: But speak all good you can devise of Caesar

speaker: BRUTUS
text: And say you do ’t by our permission,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Else shall you not have any hand at all

speaker: BRUTUS
text: About his funeral. And you shall speak

speaker: BRUTUS
text: In the same pulpit whereto I am going,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: After my speech is ended.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Be it so.

speaker: ANTONY
text: I do desire no more.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Prepare the body, then, and follow us.

speaker: ANTONY
text: O pardon me, thou bleeding piece of earth,

speaker: ANTONY
text: That I am meek and gentle with these butchers.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Thou art the ruins of the noblest man

speaker: ANTONY
text: That ever livèd in the tide of times.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Woe to the hand that shed this costly blood!

speaker: ANTONY
text: Over thy wounds now do I prophesy

speaker: ANTONY
text: (Which like dumb mouths do ope their ruby lips

speaker: ANTONY
text: To beg the voice and utterance of my tongue)

speaker: ANTONY
text: A curse shall light upon the limbs of men;

speaker: ANTONY
text: Domestic fury and fierce civil strife

speaker: ANTONY
text: Shall cumber all the parts of Italy;

speaker: ANTONY
text: Blood and destruction shall be so in use

speaker: ANTONY
text: And dreadful objects so familiar

speaker: ANTONY
text: That mothers shall but smile when they behold

speaker: ANTONY
text: Their infants quartered with the hands of war,

speaker: ANTONY
text: All pity choked with custom of fell deeds;

speaker: ANTONY
text: And Caesar’s spirit, ranging for revenge,

speaker: ANTONY
text: With Ate by his side come hot from hell,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Shall in these confines with a monarch’s voice

speaker: ANTONY
text: Cry “Havoc!” and let slip the dogs of war,

speaker: ANTONY
text: That this foul deed shall smell above the earth

speaker: ANTONY
text: With carrion men groaning for burial.

speaker: ANTONY
text: You serve Octavius Caesar, do you not?

speaker: SERVANT
text: I do, Mark Antony.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Caesar did write for him to come to Rome.

speaker: SERVANT
text: He did receive his letters and is coming,

speaker: SERVANT
text: And bid me say to you by word of mouth—

speaker: SERVANT
text: O Caesar!

speaker: ANTONY
text: Thy heart is big. Get thee apart and weep.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Passion, I see, is catching, for mine eyes,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Seeing those beads of sorrow stand in thine,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Began to water. Is thy master coming?

speaker: SERVANT
text: He lies tonight within seven leagues of Rome.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Post back with speed and tell him what hath chanced.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Here is a mourning Rome, a dangerous Rome,

speaker: ANTONY
text: No Rome of safety for Octavius yet.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Hie hence and tell him so.—Yet stay awhile;

speaker: ANTONY
text: Thou shalt not back till I have borne this corpse

speaker: ANTONY
text: Into the marketplace. There shall I try,

speaker: ANTONY
text: In my oration, how the people take

speaker: ANTONY
text: The cruel issue of these bloody men,

speaker: ANTONY
text: According to the which thou shalt discourse

speaker: ANTONY
text: To young Octavius of the state of things.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Lend me your hand.
